specialties.name: Psychiatrist
specialties.blurb: Medical doctors specialising in the diagnosis, treatment and prescribing for mental health conditions.

specialties.name: Psychologist
specialties.blurb: Registered practitioners providing assessment and evidence-based talking therapies for mental health and wellbeing.

specialties.name: ADHD GP
specialties.blurb: GPs with a special interest in assessing, diagnosing and managing adult ADHD, including prescribing where appropriate.

specialties.name: Online GP
specialties.blurb: Australian GPs providing general medical care, prescriptions and referrals via secure telehealth.

specialties.name: Men's Health GP
specialties.blurb: GPs focused on men's health — testosterone, sexual health, hair loss and preventive care.

specialties.name: Women's Health GP
specialties.blurb: GPs focused on women's health — contraception, menopause, PCOS, fertility and preventive care.

specialties.name: Weight Loss GP
specialties.blurb: GPs providing evidence-based weight management, including medication where clinically appropriate.

specialties.name: Dietitian
specialties.blurb: Accredited practising dietitians providing personalised nutrition and dietary support via telehealth.

specialties.name: Sexual Health GP
specialties.blurb: GPs providing discreet sexual health care — STI testing, contraception and treatment via telehealth.

specialties.name: Sleep GP
specialties.blurb: GPs with a special interest in insomnia and sleep disorders, offering assessment and management via telehealth.

conditions.name: ADHD
conditions.summary: Attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) is a common neurodevelopmental condition affecting attention, impulse control and activity levels. In several Australian states, appropriately trained GPs can now assess and manage adult ADHD.

sections.body: ADHD is a neurodevelopmental condition that affects how the brain regulates attention, impulsivity and activity. It often continues from childhood into adulthood, where it can affect work, study and relationships.

A diagnosis involves a structured clinical assessment against DSM-5 criteria, a developmental history, and screening for co-existing conditions. In some states, appropriately trained GPs can now assess and manage adult ADHD, shortening waits and reducing cost.
Management may include medication, psychological strategies, coaching and lifestyle support. Ongoing reviews help tailor treatment over time.

faqs.q: Can a GP diagnose ADHD in Australia?
faqs.a: In several states, appropriately trained GPs can assess, diagnose and manage adult ADHD, including prescribing where clinically appropriate. Rules vary by state.

faqs.q: How long does an ADHD assessment take?
faqs.a: A GP-led pathway is often completed within a couple of weeks, compared with 6-18 months for many private psychiatrists.

conditions.name: Anxiety
conditions.summary: Anxiety disorders involve persistent, excessive worry or fear that interferes with daily life. They are common and highly treatable with therapy, and sometimes medication.

sections.body: Occasional anxiety is normal, but an anxiety disorder involves worry or fear that is persistent, out of proportion, and affects everyday functioning. Common types include generalised anxiety, social anxiety and panic disorder.
Evidence-based psychological therapies such as CBT are first-line. Psychologists provide these, and GPs or psychiatrists can advise on medication where appropriate.

faqs.q: Do I need a referral to see a psychologist for anxiety?
faqs.a: You can see a psychologist without a referral, though a GP Mental Health Treatment Plan may give you access to Medicare rebates for a number of sessions.
Yes. Talking therapies and GP support for anxiety are well suited to telehealth for most people.

conditions.name: Depression
conditions.summary: Depression is a common mood condition involving persistent low mood, loss of interest, and changes in energy, sleep and concentration. It is treatable with therapy, lifestyle support and, where needed, medication.

sections.body: Depression is more than feeling down. It involves a persistent low mood or loss of interest lasting two weeks or more, often with changes to sleep, appetite, energy and concentration.
Psychologists provide evidence-based talking therapies, while GPs and psychiatrists can coordinate care and medication. Telehealth makes support accessible from anywhere in Australia.

faqs.a: Yes. Many people effectively manage depression with telehealth therapy and GP support, though anyone in crisis should seek urgent in-person help or call Lifeline on 13 11 14.

conditions.name: Insomnia
conditions.summary: Insomnia is difficulty falling or staying asleep that affects daytime functioning. It is common and often improves with behavioural strategies and, where appropriate, short-term medical support.

sections.body: Insomnia can be triggered by stress, anxiety, pain, shift work, caffeine and screen habits. It often becomes self-sustaining through worry about sleep itself.
Cognitive behavioural therapy for insomnia (CBT-i) is the recommended first-line treatment. A GP can review contributing factors and, where suitable, discuss short-term options.

faqs.q: What is the first-line treatment for insomnia?
faqs.a: CBT-i (cognitive behavioural therapy for insomnia) is the recommended first-line treatment, ahead of sleeping tablets, which are generally for short-term use only.

conditions.name: Menopause
conditions.summary: Menopause is the natural end of menstrual periods, usually between 45 and 55. Symptoms like hot flushes, sleep disturbance and mood changes can be managed with lifestyle measures and, for many, menopausal hormone therapy.

sections.body: Perimenopause is the transition leading up to menopause, when hormone levels fluctuate and symptoms often begin. Menopause is confirmed after 12 months without a period.
Options range from lifestyle changes to menopausal hormone therapy (MHT). A GP with an interest in women's health can help you weigh the benefits and risks for your situation.

faqs.q: Can menopause be managed via telehealth?
faqs.a: Yes. Many aspects of menopause assessment and management, including discussing MHT, can be done via telehealth with an appropriate GP.

conditions.name: PCOS
conditions.summary: Polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) is a common hormonal condition affecting periods, fertility, skin and weight. It is manageable with a combination of lifestyle, medication and ongoing care.

sections.body: PCOS involves a combination of irregular periods, signs of raised androgens, and/or polycystic ovaries on ultrasound. It is one of the most common hormonal conditions in women of reproductive age.
Management is individualised and may include lifestyle support, medication for periods or symptoms, and fertility care when relevant. A women's health GP can coordinate this.

faqs.a: PCOS can affect ovulation and fertility, but many women with PCOS conceive, sometimes with support. Early advice from a GP or fertility service helps.

conditions.name: Erectile Dysfunction
conditions.summary: Erectile dysfunction (ED) is difficulty getting or keeping an erection. It is common, often treatable, and can be an early sign of other health issues worth checking.

sections.body: ED becomes more common with age but is not an inevitable part of ageing. Causes can be physical (such as cardiovascular or hormonal factors), psychological, or a mix.
Because ED can be an early marker of heart or metabolic problems, a check-up is worthwhile. Effective treatments exist and a discreet telehealth consult is a common starting point.

faqs.q: Can I get ED treatment through telehealth?
faqs.a: Yes. A telehealth GP can assess ED, screen for underlying causes and discuss treatment discreetly, arranging any tests needed.

conditions.name: Low Testosterone
conditions.summary: Low testosterone can cause fatigue, low mood, reduced libido and other symptoms. Diagnosis requires blood tests, and treatment is guided by symptoms and results.

sections.body: Symptoms can include low energy, reduced libido, mood changes and loss of muscle mass. Levels naturally decline with age, and other health factors can contribute.
Diagnosis needs morning blood tests, usually repeated, alongside a clinical review. Testosterone therapy is only appropriate in specific, confirmed cases and requires monitoring.

faqs.a: It requires blood tests (usually early morning and repeated) interpreted alongside your symptoms by a doctor. A single low result is not enough on its own.

conditions.name: Weight Management
conditions.summary: Sustainable weight management combines nutrition, activity, behaviour and, for some people, medical support. A GP or dietitian can help build a safe, evidence-based plan.

sections.body: Weight is influenced by many factors beyond willpower, including genetics, hormones, sleep, stress and medications. Effective plans address the whole picture.
For some people, medication is clinically appropriate alongside lifestyle change. A GP can assess suitability, discuss risks and benefits, and monitor progress.

faqs.q: Are weight-loss medications right for me?
faqs.a: They suit some people but not others. A GP assesses your health, discusses benefits and risks, and only prescribes where clinically appropriate and monitored.

conditions.name: Hair Loss
conditions.summary: Hair loss is common and has many causes. Several evidence-based treatments can slow or improve it, and earlier treatment tends to work better.

sections.body: Male and female pattern hair loss is the most common cause, but thyroid problems, iron deficiency, stress and some medications can also contribute.
Evidence-based options include topical and oral treatments. A GP can identify the likely cause, check for contributing factors and discuss what may help.

faqs.q: Can hair loss be treated via telehealth?
faqs.a: Yes. A telehealth GP can assess pattern hair loss, arrange any tests, and discuss treatment options.

conditions.name: Contraception
conditions.summary: There are many contraception options, each with different benefits. A GP can help you choose one that fits your health, preferences and lifestyle.

sections.body: Options include the pill, long-acting reversible contraception (LARC), and others. The best choice depends on your health, how reliable you need it to be, and personal preference.
Many contraceptive options can be discussed and prescribed via telehealth, with in-person appointments arranged for procedures like implants or IUDs.

faqs.q: Can I get the pill through telehealth?
faqs.a: Yes, in many cases a telehealth GP can review your history and prescribe a suitable pill, or arrange an in-person visit for other methods.

conditions.name: Migraine
conditions.summary: Migraine is a common neurological condition causing recurrent, often disabling headaches. Both acute treatments and preventive strategies can reduce its impact.

sections.body: Migraine is more than a bad headache. Attacks can involve throbbing pain, nausea, and sensitivity to light and sound, sometimes with an aura beforehand.
Management includes identifying triggers, acute treatments for attacks, and preventive options for frequent migraine. A GP can help build a plan and refer if needed.

faqs.q: When should I see a doctor about migraine?
faqs.a: See a doctor if headaches are frequent, worsening, or affecting your life, or if you have new or unusual symptoms. A GP can assess and guide treatment.

questions.question: Can you get a bulk-billed ADHD assessment in Australia?
questions.answer: A fully bulk-billed (free) adult ADHD assessment is rare in Australia, because the appointments are long and complex and no single Medicare item covers them in full. More affordable pathways exist: in several states, trained GPs can now assess adult ADHD for far less than a private psychiatrist, and some parts of the work-up (such as blood tests and an ECG) are often bulk-billed.

questions.question: Do I need a referral to see a psychiatrist?
questions.answer: Yes. In Australia you generally need a referral from a GP to see a psychiatrist and to claim the Medicare rebate. The GP visit is also useful for coordinating your care and considering whether a psychologist or GP-led pathway might suit you.

questions.question: What's the difference between a psychologist and a psychiatrist?
questions.answer: A psychiatrist is a medical doctor who can diagnose mental health conditions and prescribe medication. A psychologist is a registered practitioner who provides assessment and evidence-based talking therapies but does not prescribe. Many people see both, and a GP can help you decide where to start.

questions.question: How do I get a Mental Health Care Plan?
questions.answer: A GP can prepare a Mental Health Treatment Plan after assessing you. It can give you access to Medicare rebates for a set number of psychology sessions each year. You don't need one to see a psychologist, but it reduces the out-of-pocket cost.

questions.question: Can a GP prescribe antidepressants?
questions.answer: Yes. GPs commonly assess and prescribe antidepressants and can review how they're working over time. For complex or treatment-resistant cases, a GP may involve or refer to a psychiatrist.

questions.question: Are telehealth appointments covered by Medicare?
questions.answer: Some telehealth appointments attract a Medicare rebate depending on the service, your eligibility and whether you have an existing relationship with the practice. Many telehealth clinics are privately billed. Always check the fees and any rebate with the provider before booking.

questions.question: Can I get a medical certificate online?
questions.answer: Yes. An Australian telehealth GP can assess you and, where clinically appropriate, issue a medical certificate for work or study. The doctor still needs to make a genuine clinical assessment before providing one.

questions.question: Can I get a repeat prescription online?
questions.answer: Often, yes. A telehealth GP can review your history and, where safe and appropriate, provide a repeat prescription. Some medications require an in-person review or additional monitoring first.

questions.question: Is online therapy as effective as in-person?
questions.answer: For many common concerns like anxiety and depression, evidence shows telehealth talking therapy can be as effective as in-person for a lot of people. Some situations still benefit from face-to-face care, and a clinician can advise on what suits you.

questions.question: Can a GP diagnose anxiety?
questions.answer: Yes. GPs regularly assess and diagnose anxiety, discuss treatment options including therapy and medication, and can prepare a Mental Health Treatment Plan or refer to a psychologist or psychiatrist if needed.

questions.question: How long does it take to see a psychiatrist in Australia?
questions.answer: Wait times vary widely and can range from a few weeks to many months, particularly for private psychiatrists. For some conditions, a GP-led pathway or a psychologist can be a faster starting point while you wait.

questions.question: Can a telehealth doctor order blood tests?
questions.answer: Yes. A telehealth GP can issue a pathology referral for blood tests, which you have done at a collection centre. The doctor then reviews the results with you and advises on next steps.

questions.question: What can't be treated by telehealth?
questions.answer: Telehealth suits many concerns, but anything needing a physical examination, a procedure, or urgent in-person care is not appropriate for telehealth. Emergencies always need 000 or an emergency department. A good telehealth service will tell you when you need to be seen in person.

questions.question: Can I get menopause treatment without seeing a specialist?
questions.answer: Often, yes. Many women manage menopause with a GP, including discussing and starting menopausal hormone therapy where appropriate. A GP can refer to a specialist if your situation is complex.

questions.question: How much does an online GP appointment cost?
questions.answer: Costs vary by clinic. Some online GP services are privately billed with a set fee, while others may offer a Medicare rebate if you're eligible. Check the fee and any rebate before you book so there are no surprises.

providers.name: Dr Morgan Lee
